title: AI in the loop: auditing 8,600 recipes with agent fan-out
description: How I rebuilt the CookJunkie content-quality pipeline around ten Claude agents running in parallel, with a dry-run mode, a review step, and zero direct writes to production data.
bodyMd:
## The shape of the problem

After migrating ~8,600 recipes from WordPress to MongoDB, the content was *mostly* clean. The 5% that wasn't was the hard 5% — HTML fragments in ingredient lists, missing yield fields, steps that were one paragraph instead of a list, random stray ` `s.

Doing it by hand was dumb. Doing it with a single-pass LLM prompt across 8,600 docs was also dumb (no batching, no review, no rollback).

So I built a pipeline.

## Fan-out, review, apply

```
scripts/
  audit:dump          → write per-ID source files
  audit:batch         → split into N batches
  audit:run           → fan out to K agents in parallel
  audit:update        → apply fix files (with --dry-run)
```

The trick is that **the agents don't touch the database.** They write *fix files* — one per recipe, with just the changed fields. A separate `audit:update` step walks the fix directory and applies it, with a dry-run mode that diffs against current state before anything persists.

That separation is the whole trick:

- The agents can be rerun, killed, or replaced without losing work.
- The diff is reviewable as a plain `git diff`.
- Rollback is `git revert` + re-run `audit:update`.

## Ten agents in parallel

Each batch run spawns ten Claude subagents with the same system prompt and disjoint slices of the work. They return structured JSON. I throttle the fan-out to keep wall-clock cost reasonable, but parallelism matters — serial mode took hours, fan-out takes minutes.

## The lesson

**AI works best when it's a producer, not a committer.** Give it a narrow, well-specified output format, separate the generation step from the application step, and make every step recoverable. The fun parts of the pipeline were the parts that had nothing to do with the model: the batching, the fix-file convention, the dry-run diff.

The model is the cheap part. The ops tooling around it is where the value lives.
